purpose.title: Purpose
purpose.body:
## Purpose

Use this pack for software projects in any language, framework, or domain.

The pack is language-agnostic and framework-agnostic. It adds technical execution standards, verification rules, and DDD-ADIV defaults to the core runtime.

recommended_files.title: Recommended Files
recommended_files.body:
## Recommended Files

- `DOCS.json`
- `WORKFLOW.json`
- `team/*.json`
- `STATE.json`
- `_PLAN.json`
- `TODO.json`
- `BUGS.json`
- `BUGS-SOLVED.json`
- `CHANGELOG.json` when `changelog.enabled` is not `no`
- `DECISIONS.json`
- `PIPELINE-CONFIG.json`
- `context-docs/TECHNICAL.json`
- `context-docs/FROZEN_LAYERS.json`
- `_PROMPTS.md` for raw prompt intake when the user wants a prompt queue.
- `_PROMPT-QUEUE.json` for defragmented prompt work.
- `_INSTRUCTIONS.md` for raw scratch intake only
- `_INSTRUCTION-QUEUE.json`
- generated indexes and diagnostics as `.json`

Read `DOCS.json` before reading, creating, renaming, or updating project documents so the agent chooses the correct source and write target.
- `tools/`

required_reads_for_code_projects.title: Required Reads for Code Projects
required_reads_for_code_projects.body:
## Required Reads for Code Projects

- `core/AGENT-CONTRACT.json`
- `core/FILE-FORMATS.json`
- `core/PROMOTION-RULES.json`
- `core/CONTEXT-COMPACTION.json`
- `project/code/WORKFLOW.json`
- `project/code/BRANCH-MODE.json` when `workflow.branch_mode` is `branch_scoped`
- `project/code/COMMANDS.json`
- `project/code/REGISTRIES.json`
- `project/code/DDD-ADIV.json`
- `project/code/GIT.json`
- `project/code/FROZEN-LAYERS.json`
- `project/code/INSTRUCTION-CAPTURE.json`
- `project/code/DIAGNOSTICS.json`
- `core/PROJECT-PROFILE.json` when `project_profile` exists
- `core/ROI-BIAS.json`
- `core/SCRIPT-FALLBACK.json`
- `core/TASK-WORK.json` for broad multi-step tasks
- `WORKFLOW.json` when priority steering, feature triage, planning, or broad project-risk judgment matters
- `team/*.json` only when the user names a role, asks for team-style review, or `WORKFLOW.json` routes the task to that role

technical_defaults.title: Technical Defaults
technical_defaults.body:
## Technical Defaults

- Prefer readable, diagnosable code over compact or clever code.
- Prefer explicit control flow, explicit data flow, explicit ownership, and explicit state transitions.
- Separate pure domain logic from I/O, framework glue, persistence, engine APIs, and other side effects.
- Normalize external errors at boundaries.
- Prefer fail-fast behavior for invalid states.
- Use guard clauses and small functions over deep nesting.
- Keep parameter counts and cyclomatic complexity under control.
- Use typed, linted, formatter-friendly code when the ecosystem supports it.
- Avoid hidden semantics, ambiguous convention, and runtime-only meaning when a mechanical contract is possible.
- Before non-obvious source-code writes, run a lightweight web research spike for established patterns, domain archetypes, and known failure modes unless the active plan already names a research-backed mechanism. Ask concise scale/dynamism questions first when the architecture depends on future load.
- Before implementation, ask whether a small better-version-now step should be included: clearer name, small boundary, named invariant, explicit contract, safer data ownership, security check, migration path, diagnostic hook, or verification seam. Default to doing it when it is cheap now and likely expensive later.
- Make durable habits convenient by default: when a convention or process would otherwise require repeated willpower, prefer a helper, script, template, schema, lint/check gate, preset, or UI/process affordance that carries the contract automatically. Treat willpower-only techniques as unlikely to survive six months of normal work without burnout.
- Scale that default by profile: in legacy or surgical work, prefer the smallest safe local improvement and do not modernize unrelated architecture.
- Organize code top-down when practical: entry points and workflow first, lower-level helpers later.
- Keep one block focused on one concern and one abstraction level.
- Extract intent-revealing helpers when a block starts doing multiple jobs.
- Prefer explicit comparisons and conditions over coercive, ambiguous, or clever expressions.
- Prefer structured APIs and parsers over ad hoc string manipulation when a reasonable option exists.
- Isolate nondeterminism and external boundaries behind small, testable interfaces when practical.
- Apply lightweight futureproof before choosing the implementation model. If behavior will likely become batch, hot-path, storage-heavy, I/O-heavy, framework-heavy, count-heavy, dynamic, or repeatedly exercised, prefer an appropriate bulk/query/index/cache/queue/helper/pooling/activation contract over making convenient per-object framework usage the broad path.
- Comments should explain non-obvious constraints, workarounds, hazards, or tradeoffs; do not restate visible code.

script_first_automation_directive.title: Script-First Automation Directive
script_first_automation_directive.body:
## Script-First Automation Directive

Use existing project scripts and pipeline tools whenever practical.

This is a high-priority programming directive: when a script can safely do the work, prefer the script.

Before manually performing repeatable work, check `tools/`, package scripts, task runners, Makefiles, CI commands, framework CLIs, and project-specific helper scripts.

Prefer scripts for:

- initialization,
- audits,
- quality gates,
- formatting,
- tests,
- diagnostics,
- snapshots,
- commits,
- migrations,
- generated indexes,
- documentation normalization.

If a suitable script exists, use it instead of reimplementing the same workflow manually.

If a script is skipped, state why: missing dependency, unsafe side effect, wrong scope, platform mismatch, or user instruction.

Run status, check, audit, and quality scripts at verification boundaries instead of after every micro-edit.

For the normal task flow, gather context first, make the code and document edits for the current scope, then run one git-based batch finalizer near the end. The finalizer should discover changed files from git, normalize text, rebuild document caches, refresh code-line indexes, run checks, and report status through repository scripts instead of requiring manual normalizer/cache/index command chains.

Prefer `tools/repo/finalize_task_check_commit_push.ps1 -Message "<message>"` for Git-backed task closure when available.
If `tools/repo/finalize_task_check_commit_push.ps1` or a lower-level finalizer fails on a repeatable mechanical issue, fix or extend the relevant script so future equivalent work is handled automatically before rerunning the finalizer.

For compact changed-path checks during a verification boundary, prefer a helper such as:

```powershell
powershell -NoProfile -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File tools/repo/check_task.ps1 -Paths <changed paths>
```

For structured project documents, generated files, registries, or contract-managed files, prefer repository scripts over manual edits when a relevant script exists.

For item moves between structured JSON documents, use `tools/documents/migrate_document_item.ps1`.

For item ordering inside one structured JSON document, use `tools/documents/move_document_item.ps1`.

For controlled insertion into structured JSON documents, use `tools/documents/insert_document_item.ps1`.

For one-line list additions inside an existing structured JSON item, use `tools/documents/insert_checked_document_list_item.ps1` when available so insertion and compact changed-path verification run as one workflow.

For candidate-title duplicate review before documentation updates, use `tools/documents/review_document_list_candidates.ps1` when available so duplicate search and candidate section reads run as one workflow.

For repeated or structurally constrained edits without a script, complete the requested change first, then recommend adding a small script when it would reduce future drift.

ddd_adiv_recommendation.title: DDD-ADIV Recommendation
ddd_adiv_recommendation.body:
## DDD-ADIV Recommendation

Use DDD-ADIV as the default design lens:

- model domain concepts explicitly,
- isolate bounded contexts,
- protect invariants close to the state they govern,
- record durable architecture decisions,
- verify risky behavior with tests, checks, or clear manual validation.

The full definition lives in `project/code/DDD-ADIV.json`.

For legacy or surgical zones, use DDD-ADIV as a lens rather than a forced migration: keep the legacy shape stable, enforce repeated behavior through helpers/adapters/seams/checks, and reserve full DDD-ADIV boundaries for non-legacy, actively modernized, or explicitly extracted code.

known_solution_bias.title: Known-Solution Bias
known_solution_bias.body:
## Known-Solution Bias

Before custom implementation, apply the full code known-solutions directive:

- Known solutions check: identify whether established patterns, proven practices, standard tools, framework conventions, or business/software archetypes solve the problem well enough.
- Web research spike check: for non-obvious write-code work, quickly research archetypes and known failure modes before choosing a custom design; record the named implementation mechanism in the plan or answer before editing when the work is structural.
- Architecture archetypes check: compare the problem against recognizable structures such as layered boundaries, adapters, state machines, queues, policies, repositories, domain services, transaction scripts, and anti-corruption layers.
- Build-vs-borrow discipline: avoid bespoke systems when a framework feature, library, workflow, or pattern is cheaper, clearer, and easier to maintain.
- Better-version-now discipline: optimize by default for future correctness when the extra step is modest; pause when it becomes a broad refactor, speculative abstraction, or profile mismatch.
- Lightweight-futureproof check: if work will plausibly scale by row count, object count, event frequency, generated content, request volume, file size, query count, realtime actors, dynamic branches/modules, or background jobs, prefer the known lightweight pattern for that scale before defaulting to per-object framework convenience.
- Profile-fit check: local patterns and project philosophy shape implementation after research, but they do not automatically win over a known domain failure mode. If research and local style conflict, ask with the concrete tradeoff.
- DDD alignment: use patterns to clarify domain ownership, invariants, boundaries, and language; do not add pattern ceremony that obscures the domain.

Examples by domain:

- PHP/CRUD/business: validation, authorization checks, transaction boundaries, idempotency keys, audit logs, schema migrations, explicit DTO/request shapes, N+1 avoidance, bulk queries only when the operation is genuinely batch or measured as hot.
- Frontend/business UI: component boundaries that match user workflows, adapter layers around legacy APIs, explicit state ownership, accessibility-safe controls, and minimal change inside old framework templates.
- Realtime/gamedev: frame budgets, activation radii, pooling, batched presentation, spatial indexes, data-first world state, and engine physics/render objects only for active local work.
- Generic backend: queues, caching, retry/backoff, contract tests, integration adapters, background jobs, and bounded resource usage.

Use the standard solution when it is cheaper and clearer than bespoke architecture.

If rejecting a known solution, state the constraint that makes it a poor fit.

task_type_routing.title: Task Type Routing
task_type_routing.body:
## Task Type Routing

For programming projects, classify the request before reading widely or editing.

The default language/framework-agnostic route is:

1. Identify whether the task is feature implementation, bug analysis, diagnostics/log analysis, performance analysis, audit, vision/product discussion, document update, plan update, plan analysis, or vision/architecture analysis.
2. Load the smallest context for that task type: current state, workflow only when priority steering matters, active plan/backlog when sequencing matters, bug/diagnostic registries when evidence matters, and the smallest owning source/context slice before code edits.
3. Apply code standards through the project knowledge layer when available.
4. Execute through the owning boundary, using standard framework/library mechanisms before custom infrastructure when they fit.
5. Verify with a task-appropriate gate and update only the memory layers whose current truth changed.

If the request does not fit a known task type, stop before broad reads or edits, name the ambiguity, propose a concrete workflow, and ask for approval unless the work is small, local, reversible, and low-risk.

profile_aware_defaults.title: Profile-Aware Defaults
profile_aware_defaults.body:
## Profile-Aware Defaults

Use `project_profile` to choose the right kind of known solution and the right amount of future-proofing.

For `legacy` or `sunset` projects:

- surgical bugfix means surgical change,
- match surrounding code and framework style,
- do not introduce a new paradigm inside a narrow ticket,
- add small safety improvements only when they directly reduce risk,
- use helper contracts, adapters, seams, diagnostics, or checks to make correct behavior convenient without forcing a new paradigm,
- put larger modernization into a separate explicit task.

For `greenfield` or `early` projects:

- prefer clear ownership boundaries,
- add contracts, schemas, tests, diagnostics, and DDD-ADIV names before complexity grows,
- make the correct path the convenient/default path through helpers, templates, scripts, presets, or checks,
- use better-version-now strongly when the added cost is small.

For `none` or `smoke-only` test coverage:

- propose a golden master test, manual reproduction script, or fixture snapshot before non-trivial behavior changes,
- keep changes small and reversible,
- report unverified assumptions explicitly.

For `custom in-house` or `mixed` frameworks:

- isolate unstable or external boundaries behind adapters,
- search for injection seams,
- follow local framework conventions for local changes,
- avoid editing framework core unless that is the explicit task.

For mature standard frameworks:

- prefer built-in framework mechanisms,
- follow ecosystem conventions,
- avoid custom infrastructure when standard extension points fit.

change_strategy.title: Change Strategy
change_strategy.body:
## Change Strategy

- For surgical fixes, make the smallest safe change.
- For larger work, introduce a clear ownership boundary.
- Preserve coherent architecture.
- Do not refactor unrelated areas.
- Remove dead code, unused imports, duplicate conditions, and temporary diagnostics when touched.
- Treat syntax errors, type errors, unresolved references, and linter failures as must-fix issues.
- When existing architecture is coherent, extend it instead of replacing it.
- When existing architecture is incoherent, prefer one explicit boundary repair over scattered local workarounds.
- Do not introduce abstractions for vague future needs.
- Add an abstraction only when it removes real complexity, reduces meaningful duplication, or matches an established local pattern.

task_gating.title: Task Gating
task_gating.body:
## Task Gating

Before implementation, check whether the requested work is the best future-facing solution.

Before implementation, also check whether this task deserves the better version now: small explicit boundary, invariant, contract, security/data-ownership guard, migration path, diagnostic, or verification seam that would be much more expensive to retrofit later.

Before relying on a new convention, checklist, or team habit, check whether the desired behavior can be made easier through a default, helper, script, template, schema, lint/check gate, or UI/process affordance. If it requires willpower every time, treat it as a fragile process and reframe it into a convenient contract where practical.

If a cheaper prerequisite, enabling contract, diagnostic step, or architecture cleanup should happen first, recommend it before implementing or make it the first step when it stays inside the user's requested outcome.

Use a short plan-first checkpoint for work that is complex, underspecified, structural, or spans multiple ownership boundaries. For non-obvious source-code writes, that checkpoint should name the researched implementation mechanism unless the active plan already does. For non-obvious source-code writes, that checkpoint should name the researched implementation mechanism unless the active plan already does.

Before broad implementation, note likely impact when the task touches:

- multiple systems,
- state transitions,
- persistence or schemas,
- randomness, concurrency, async, timing, or scheduling,
- more than two or three files,
- public APIs, tool contracts, or registries,
- a model that may grow by large count, high frequency, large payload, or repeated batch work.

When scale is unknown and architecture depends on it, ask for the intended load shape before researching or implementing: rough active count, total count, batch size, update frequency, persistence volume, dynamic variation, module/branch count, and expected growth order such as 2x, 10x, or 100x.

Stop and reframe when the requested approach would materially increase coupling, hide side effects, add nondeterminism, blur ownership, or lock a predictable-scale system into a heavyweight model that will likely need replacement.

Stop and reframe when the requested approach would knowingly create avoidable technical debt in a long-lived area: unclear ownership, missing contract, weak data boundary, unsafe security assumption, fragile migration path, hidden side effect, or framework convenience that history says will become the wrong abstraction.

For local, reversible, clearly scoped changes, implement directly and report the outcome.

reporting.title: Reporting
reporting.body:
## Reporting

For non-trivial code work, the first execution update should be compact and concrete:

- Usage: whether the task may be expensive in context, tool calls, or time.
- Impact: the likely blast radius.
- Domains: the touched ownership areas.
- Current situation: observed facts.
- Expected result: what should be true after the change.
- Applied solutions: known solutions, scripts, or patterns to use.

After implementation, report changed files, verification, skipped checks, and residual risk.

review_heuristics.title: Review Heuristics
review_heuristics.body:
## Review Heuristics

Lead with bugs, regressions, security issues, data-loss risks, and missing verification.

Flag:

- unsafe dynamic execution,
- injection risks,
- unintended mass updates or deletes,
- swallowed exceptions,
- inconsistent return paths,
- hidden control flow,
- concurrency hazards,
- unbounded resource usage,
- unclear ownership.

verification.title: Verification
verification.body:
## Verification

Use the strongest practical quality gate available:

- unit tests,
- integration tests,
- typecheck,
- lint,
- formatter check,
- build,
- smoke test,
- manual reproduction,
- diff check.

If verification is impossible or skipped, report it explicitly.
